entry.title: Optimization
entry.content:
Optimization

we are looking for the min or max of a function on a specific domain. Generally, optimization problems > convex problems > linear programming problems

if $X^* \in \mathbb{R}$ such that $f(x^*)\geq f(n), \forall n \in \mathbb{R}$

Convex problems

An optimization problem is convex to find $min(f(x)), x\in D$, $D$ is a convex set and $f$ is a convex function. In brief, finding a local min or global min is enough

Convex sets

Is it always possible to draw a line between 2 points of my set, all the lines are also included in the set

Definition: $S$ is a shape in $R^2$, S is convex if $\forall x,y\in S,\forall t \in (0,1), tx+(1-t)y \in S$

Convex functions

A function $f:D \in R^n \to R$ is convex if

  • $D$ is a convex set

  • $f(tx+(1-t)y) \le tf(x)+(1-t)f(y),\forall t \in(0,1),\forall x,y\in D$

means that the curve representing $f$ is always below the line joining 2 points of the curve

**N.B.: **

  • in a convex function, a local min is the global min
  • In a convex function, the gradient is an incremental ascent

Linear programming problem

$-f(x) = f(-x)$

$f(\lambda x)= \lambda f(x)$
$$
\begin{cases}
ax+by =c_1\\
mx+by = c_2
\end{cases}
$$
$a,b,c_1,c_2$ are constraints of this linear equation.

The area contributed by the feasible solutions(where all constraints are satisfied) is called the feasible region.

Simplex method

If we deal with an LPP which is a maximization problem with a non-negativity constraint, we can use the simplex method.

entry.title: Diagonalization
entry.content:
Diagonalisation

An n × n matrix A is diagonalisable if it is similar to a diagonal matrix: that is if there exists an invertible n × n matrix C and a diagonal matrix D such that $A= CDC^{−1}$.

Compute powers of a diagonalisable matrix. If $A= CDC^{−1}$, where $D$ is a diagonal matrix, then $A^n = CD^nC^{−1}$:
$$
A = C
\begin{bmatrix}
x &0 &0\\
0 &y &0\\
0 &0 &z
\end{bmatrix}
C^{-1}
\iff
A^n = C
\begin{bmatrix}
x^n &0 &0\\
0 &y^n &0\\
0 &0 &z^n
\end{bmatrix}
C^{-1}
$$

entry.title: Possibility
entry.content:
Intro

Possibility: calculating the likelihood of future events

Statistics: gathering, and analyzing data

Machine learning: models which are accurate most of the time. It contains 2 important factors, accuracy (how close the predictions are from the true value) and robustness (the ability to generalize to unknown data)

Possibility

Sample space ($\Omega$) is a set of all possible outcomes or results of a random experiment.

Points $\omega$ in $\Omega$ are called sample outcomes, realizations, or elements.

Subsets of $\Omega$ are called Events.

$e.g.$:

if $\Omega = {1,2,3,4,5,6}$, A= ‘Even result’=${2,4,6}$ is an event.

Let $S$ be a sample space. A probability on $S$ is a real-valued function $P$, $P:{\text{Events}}\to \mathbb{R}$.

satisfying:

  1. $P(A)≥0$ for any event $A$.

  2. $P(S)=1$.

  3. If $A1, A2, . . .$ are mutually exclusive events (m.e.e) then $P\left(\displaystyle \bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty} A_i\right) = \displaystyle \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} P(A_i)$

    whereby m.e.e. we mean $A_i∩A_j =\emptyset$ when $i\neq j$. ($\emptyset$ means empty set)

Basic properties of probability:

  1. $P(∅)=0$.

  2. $P(A’)=1−P(A)$.

  3. If $A\subset B$ then $P(A)≤P(B)$. In particular, if $B$ is $S$, we get $0 ≤ P(A) ≤ 1$ for any event $A$.

  4. Let $B_1,B_2,…$ be m.e.e. such that $S=\left(\displaystyle \bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty} B_i\right)$, that is, the $B_i$’s form a partition of $S$.

    Then for any event $A$, $P(A) = \displaystyle \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} P(A\cap B_i)$

  5. that for any event $B$, since $S = B \cup B’$ is a partition of $S$, then $P(A) = P(A \cap B) + P(A \cap B’)$.

    In particular, since $A \cap B’ = A \setminus B$ (They are two different ways to express {in A but not in B}), we have $P(A \setminus B) = P(A) − P(A \cap B)$.

  6. For any events $A$ and $B$, $P (A \cup B) = P (A) + P (B) − P (A \cap B)$.

  7. If $A,B$ are independent events of $\Omega \iff P(A \cap B) = P(A)\cdot P(B)$

Marginalisation

$P(x=x_i) =\displaystyle \sum_{j=1}^n P(x=x_i,y=y_j)$

Mean, variance, standard deviation

$\mu = E[x] =\displaystyle \sum_{i=1}^nx_iP_i$

$Var(x) = E[(x-\mu)^2]=E[(x-E[x])^2] = E[x^2-2xE[x]+(E[x])^2]=E[x^2]-2E[x]E[x]+(E[x])^2=E[x^2]-(E[x])^2=E[x^2]-\mu^2$

Bernoulli Distribution

$Bern(x|\mu)=\mu^x(1-\mu)^{1-x}$

$Var(x)=\mu(1-\mu)$

Covariance

$Cov(x,y)=E[(x-\mu_x)(y-\mu_y)]$

N.B.:

  1. If $Cov(x,y)=0$, just means there is no linear relationship between $x$ and $y$! It doesn’t mean $x$ and $y$ are independent. eg:$x^2+y^2 = 1$
  2. If $Cov(x,y)>0$, when $x$ increase, y increase
  3. if $Cov(x,y)<0$, when x decrease, y decrease

Conditional possibility

If $A,B$ are independent events, $P(A \cap B)= P(A)\cdot P(B)$

If $A,B$ are not necessarily independent events, $P(A \cap B)=P(B|A)\cdot P(A)$ also can be written as $P(A|B) = \frac {P(A \cap B)} {P(B)}$

law of total possibility

$P(A)=P(A|B_1)P(B_1)+P(A|B_2)P(B_2)+…+P(A|B_n)P(B_n)$

If $A,B$ are independent events, $P(A | B)= \displaystyle \frac {P(A)\cdot P(B)}{P(B)}= P(A)$

Bayes Theorem

By definition of conditional probability $𝑃(𝐴\cap 𝐵) =𝑃(𝐴|𝐵)𝑃(𝐵)$

Swapping A, B and gives $P(B\cap A) =P(B|A)P(A)$ But $P(A\cap B) =P(B∩A)$, so $𝑃(𝐴|𝐵)𝑃(𝐵) =𝑃(𝐵|𝐴)𝑃(𝐴)$

Dividing both sides by $P(B)$ gives $𝑃(𝐴|𝐵) =\frac {𝑃(𝐵|𝐴)𝑃(𝐴)} {𝑃(𝐵)}
$

Probability distribution

PDF(probability density function)

Definition : $f$ is a continuous function with:

  • $f(x) \ge 0, \forall x$
  • $\int_{-x}^{x} dx = 1$
  • $P(a<x<b) = \int_a^bdx$

  1. Random variables - usually denoted by uppercase letters near the end of our alphabet （e.g. X, Y）.

  2. Particular value - now use lowercase letters, such as x, which correspond to the r.v. X.

if $f$ is a continuous function and

$P(x=a)=P(a\le x \le a)=\int_a^af(x)dx=0,a\in\mathbb{R}$
$$
P(x<=a) \equiv P(x<a)\\
proof:\\
P(x<a \cup x=a)= P(x<a)+P(a)=P(x<a)
$$
Let $X$ be a continuous random variable with associated pdf $f(x)$. Then its expectation and variance are denoted by $\mu$ and $\sigma$

which $\mu = \int_{-\infin}^{+\infin} f(x) dx$ and $\sigma = \int_{-\infin}^{+\infin} (f(x)-x)^2 dx$

Exercise:

suppose $X,Y$ both in $[0,1], f(x,y) =1$, what is the possibility of $P(X>Y)$

Solution:
$$
\begin{align*}
P(X > Y) &= \int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{y} 1 , dx , dy \
&= \int_{0}^{1} \left[\int_{0}^{y} 1 , dx\right] , dy \
&= \int_{0}^{1} y , dy \
&= \left[\frac{y^2}{2}\right]_{0}^{1} \
&= \frac{1}{2}
\end{align*}
$$

Important probability Distributions

Binomial:

The binomial distribution is a discrete probability distribution that models the number of successes in a fixed number of independent and identically distributed Bernoulli trials.

The Binomial $r.v.$ counts the total number of successes out of $n$ trials, where $X$ is the number of successes.

• Each trial must be independent of the previous experiment.

• The probability of success must be the same for each trial.

$P(X=x)=\displaystyle\frac {n!} {x!(n-x)!}p^x(1-p)^{n-x}$

Poisson

Uniform

Normal

Exponential

entry.title: Abstract Data Types
entry.content:
Abstract Data Types (ADTs)

An ADT is a tuple （T, U, O, P, A） where

  • T is the name of the ADT we are defining.

  • U is a set of （already defined） ADTs used in the definition of T. Notice that this implies a notion of a hierarchy of ADTs.

  • O is a set of operation signatures that defines the syntax for the legal use of T objects.

  • P is a set of pre-conditions （logical expressions that always must hold true） to using some of the operations in O.

  • A is a set of axioms （logical expressions that are admittedly true） defining the behaviour of the operations in O.

Operation signature

The signature of an operation is made of

  1. its name;
  2. a （possibly empty） set of ADT describing the domains for parameter values for the operation;
  3. an ADT describing the domain of the result of the operation. The ADT specifying the parameter types are called input ADT （or source ADT）, and that specifying the result is called output （or target） ADT.

For example:

ADT boolean

operations

true: -> boolean

false: -> boolean

$ !_ $: boolean -> boolean

$ _∧_ $: boolean x boolean -> boolean

$ _∨_ $: boolean x boolean -> boolean

axioms

$!true = false$

$!!a = a$

$a ∧ true=a$

$a ∧ false = false$

$a ∨b=!(!a∧ !b)$

Axioms:

$A_1: 0+x \equiv x$

$A_2: sx+y \equiv x + sy$

$A_3: 0=0 \equiv true$

$A_4: 0=sx \equiv false$

$A_5: sx=0 \equiv false$

$A_6: sx = sy \equiv x=y $

Example: Is 2 = 3? Proof with axiom
$$
egin{align*}
ss0 &= sss0…A_6\
s0 &= ss0…A_6\
0 &= s0 ightarrow false …A_3
\end{align*}
$$

ADT counter

a counter has a natural value (0 at inial time). It can only be incremented or decremented by 1 each time. Decrementing a null has no effect

Operations:

new: -> counter

increment: counter-> counter

decrement: counter-> counter

value: counter -> natural
$$
egin{align*}
&A_1: dec(new) \equiv new\
&A_2: dec(inc(c)) \equiv c… ext{both c and new are counters}\
&A_3: value(new) \equiv 0\
&A_4: value(inc(c)) \equiv value(c)+1
\end{align*}
$$
another example: $value(inc(dec(inc(new))))=value(0+1-1+1)=1$

ADT Stack

A stack in computer science can be seen as a homogeneous collection of elements for which only the topmost element is accessible. For instance, a stack of plates is almost always accessed by its top, be it to take plates before lunch or dinner, or to put them back after washing up. One commonly uses the term LIFO （for Last In, First Out） to refer to that kind of data structure:

• Tell wether the stack is empty.

• Push a new element on top of the stack.

• Pop the top element out of the stack.

• Tell what the top element is.

ADT Stack Uses Element, boolean

Operations

$create : -> Stack$

$isEmpty: Stack -> boolean$

$push:Stack\quad ext{x}\quad Element - Stack$

$pop: Stack Stack$

$top:Stack -> Element$

Preconditions

$pop（p） \iff !isEmpty（p)$

$top（p） \iff !isEmpty（p)$

Axioms

$A_1: isEmpty (create) = true$

$A_2: isEmpty (push(p,e))= false$

$A_3: top (push(p,e))= e$

$A_4: pop(push(p,e)) = p$

ADT Queue

A queue is an homogeneous collection of elements accessed by one of the two ends of the structure: insertion at the one end, removals at the other end.

Intuitively, it mirrors the behaviour of a real queue, such as the one that can appear at the entrance of a cinema for instance. It can be called FIFO, for First In, First Out: the first element that entered the queue is the first to leave it.

Expected functionalities are as follows:

• Tell wether the queue is empty

• Add an element to the end of the queue

• Remove the first （head） element from the queue

• Tell which is the head element

Uses boolean,Element

operations

$create: queue$

$isEmpty:queue o boolean$

$add : queue \quad ext{x}\quad Element o queue$

$remove:queue o queue$

$head:queue o Element$

pre-conditions

$remove (f, e)\iff !isEmpty(f)$

$head (f)\iff !isEmpty(f)$

axioms

$A_1:isEmpty （create） \equiv true$

$A_2: isEmpty(add(f,e)) \equiv false$

$A_3: isEmpty(f) \Rightarrow head(add(f,e))\equiv e$

$A_4: !isEmpty （f） \Rightarrow head(add(f, e))= head(f)$

$A_5: isEmpty(f) \Rightarrow remove(add(f,e))= create$

$A_6:!isEmpty(f)\Rightarrow remove(add (f, e))= add(remove (f),e)$

Tower of Hanoi

hanoi(n,from,to,use) n is the amount of disks, from is the inital stick, to is the destination stick, use is the aux stick

List ADT

Inside a list, each element is associated with its （unique） position （a strictly positive integer）. Unlike the previous two ADT, any element of the list can be accessed, whatever its position is.

The minimum functionalities expected from a basic list are as follows:

• access the top element （first） of the list

• access the rest of the list （i.e.the list minus its first element） • tell the length of the list

• access an element based on its position in the list

• add an element at a given position in the list

• remove an element at a given position in the list

As a first step, we just define the ADT with its constructors and access functions （accessors） to the frst element and to the rest of the list （the other operations can then be expressed using those basic ones):

ADT List

Uses boolean,Element,Natural operations

$create : o list$

$isEmpty: list o boolean$

$rest : list o list$

$cons : Element \quad ext{x}\quad list o list$

$first: list o Element$

pre-conditions

$first(l)\iff !isEmpty(l)$

$rest(l)\iff !isEmpty(l)$

axioms

$A_1: isEmpty（create） \equiv true$

$A_2: isEmpty (cons(e, l))\equiv false$

$A_3: first(cons(e, l))\equiv e$

$A_4:rest(cons(e, l)) \equiv l$

Minimum list extension

Based on the functions described above, we can now define all the classical operations about lists. We get the following ADT:

extension ADT list

Uses Natural

operations

$length:1ist o Natural$

$add : list \quad ext{x}\quad Natural \quad ext{x}\quad Element o list$

$remove :list \quad{x} Natural o list nth : list x Natural Element$

pre-conditions

$remove (1,n)) \iff 1 ≤n ≤ length(1)$

$n^{th}(l,n))\iff 1≤n ≤ length(1)$

$add（1,n,e））\iff 1 ≤n ≤ length（1） + 1$

axioms

$A_5: length（create） = 0$

$A_6: 1!=create \Rightarrow length(1) \equiv length(rest(1))+ 1$

$A_7: n^{th}（1,1） \equiv first(1)$

$A_8: i>1=n^{th}(1,i) \equiv n^{th}(rest(1),i-1)$

$A_9: add(1,1,e) \equiv cons(e,1)$

$A_{10}:i>1=add（l,i,e）\equiv cons (first(1),add(rest(1),i-1,e)$

$A_{11}: remove (1,1) = rest(1)$

$A_{12}: i>1 \Rightarrow remove(1,i) \equiv cons(first(1),remove (rest(1),i-1)$

Merging sorted list

ADT merged list

Use element in lists

Operation

$issorted: list o boolean$

$merge: list \quad ext{x}\quad list o list$

Precondition

$merge(l_1,l_2) \iff issorted(l_1) \quad ext{and}\quad issorted(l_2)$

Axioms

$A_1: issorted(new)\equiv T$

$A_2: issorted(cons(l,new)) \equiv T$

$A_3: issorted(cons(e,l))\equiv e<=first(l) \quad ext{and}\quad issorted(l)$

$A_4:issorted(l) \equiv first(l) <=first(rest(l)) \quad ext{and}\quad issorted(rest(l))$

$A_5: merge(new,l) \equiv l$

$A_6: merge(l,new)\equiv l$

$A_7: first(l_1)<=first(l_2) \Rightarrow merge(l_1,l_2) \equiv cons(first(l_1),merge(rest(l_1),l_2))$

$A_8: first(l_1)>first(l_2) \Rightarrow merge(l_1,l_2) \equiv cons(first(l_2),merge(rest(l_2),l_1))$

ADT indexof list

operations

$indexof: list \quad ext{x}\quad element o Natutral$

Precondition

$indexof(l,e) \iff cons(l,e)$

Axioms

$A_1: indexof(new,e)\equiv 0$

$A_2: indexof(cons(e,l),e) \equiv 1$

$A_2^*:indexof(l,first(l))\equiv 1$

$A_3: indexof(cons(e_1,l,e_2))\equiv l+indexof(l,e_2)$

$A_3^*:indexof(l,e)\equiv 1+indexof(rest(l),e)$

ADT index3(3 refers to 3 parameters in total and this function is used to check the postion of the 3^th element)

Operations

$index3: list \quad ext{x}\quad element\quad ext{x}\quad natural o natural$

Axiom

$A_1: indexof(l,e)\equiv index3(l,e,1)$

$A_2: index3(new,e,n)\equiv 0$

$A_3: index3(cons(e,l),e,n) \equiv n$

$A_4:index3(cons(e_1,l),e_2,n)\equiv index3(l,e_2,n+1)$

ADT revere list

$last((A,B,C,D)) o D$

$allbutlast((A,B,C,D)) o(A,B,C)$

Operations

$last: list o element$

$allbutlast: list o list$

$reverse: list o list$

Axioms

$A_1: last(cons(e,new))\equiv e$

$A_2: last(cons(e,l))\equiv last(l)$

$A_3: allbutlast(cons(e,new))\equiv new$

$A_4: allbutlast(cons(e,l)) \equiv cons(e,allbutlast(l))$

$A_5: reverse(new) \equiv new$

$A_6: reverse(l) \equiv cons(last(l),reverse(allbutlast(l)))$

Complexity

last:

$C(1) = 1$

$C(n) =1+C(n-1)=2+C(n-2)+…+i+C(n-i)$, suppose $i =n-1$, then we get $C(n)=n$

allbutlast:

$C(1) = 0$

$C(n)=C(n-1)+3 =C(n-2)+6 =…=C(n-i)+3(n-i)$,suppose $i = n-1$, then we get $C(n) = 3(n-1)$

reverse:

$C(0) = 0$
$$
egin{align*}
C(n) &= 1+n+3(n-1)+C(n-1)\
&=C(n-1)+4n-2\

&=C(n-2)+4(n+(n-1))-2-2\
&=C(n-3)+4(n+(n-1)+(n-2))-2-2-2\
&=…\
&=C(n-i)+4\sum_{k=n-i+1}^nk-2i\
&=C(0)+4 rac {n(n+1)} 2 -2n …. ext{(suppose i =n)}\
&=2n^2

\end{align*}
$$

Trees

A tree is composed of a node （called its root） containing information, and of a （possibly empty） set of trees （called its children）. This definition is clearly recursive, as will be most of the definitions of operations on trees.

ADT Tree

Use integer

Oporations

$new: element \cross forest o tree$

$newforest: forest$

$addtree: forest \cross tree\cross integer o forest$

$root: tree o element$

$subTrees: tree o fortest$

$ntree: forest o integer$

$nth: forest \cross interger o tree$

$remTree: forest \cross integer o forest$

Particial conditions

$nth(f,n) \iff 1\le n \le nTree$

$newTree(f,n)\iff 1\le n \le nTree$

$addTree(f,t,n) \iff 1\le n \le nTree+1$

Axioms

$A_1: root(new(e,f))\equiv e$

$A_2: subTrees(new(e,f))\equiv f$

$A_3: nTrees(newF) \equiv 0$

$A_4: nTrees(addTree(f,t,n)) \equiv nTrees(f)+1$

$A_5: a>n \Rightarrow nth(addTree(f,t,a),n) \equiv nth(f,n)$

$A_6: nth(addTree(f,t,n),n) \equiv t$

$A_7: nth(addTree(f,t,a),n) \equiv nth(f,n-1)$

Explanation: the left part says to add a new subtree to the forest, and n refers to the rank after modification. So, if to find the same subtree of the forest before modification, we need to minus 1 to eliminate the influence of rank from adding a new subtree

$A_8:a>r \Rightarrow remTree(addTree(f,t,a),r)\equiv addTree(remTree(f,r),t,a-1)$

$A_9:remTree(addTree(f,t,n),n)\equiv f$

$A_{10}: remTree(addTree(f,t,a),r)\equiv addTree(remTree(f,r-1),t,a)$

ADT extension tree, forest

Use Boolean

Operations

$contains: tree \cross element o boolean$

$containsF: forest \cross element o boolean$

Axioms

$A_1: contains(new(e,f),e) \equiv true$

$A_2:contains(t,e) \equiv containsF(subTrees(t),e)$

$A_3: containsF(newF,e)\equiv false$

$A_4:containsF(addTree(f,t,n),e) \equiv contains(t,e) \or containsF(f,e)$

Excersise:

For ADT Tree

size(t) $ o$ numbers of t

height(t) $ o$ height of t

PL(t) $ o$ pathlength of t

For ADT extension Tree, Forest

Opertaion

$size o Int$

$sizeF o Int$

$height: int$

$heightF: o int$

$PL: o int$

$PLF: o int$

Axioms

$A_1: size(new(e,f)) \equiv 1 + sizeF(f)$

$A_2: sizeF(newF) \equiv 0$

$A_3: sizeF(addTree(f,t,n))\equiv size(t)+sizeF(f)$

$A_4: height(new(e,f)) \equiv 1+height(f)$

$A_5: heightF(newF) \equiv 0$

$A_6: heightF(addTree(f,t,n)) \equiv max(height(t),heightF(f))$

$A_7: PL(new(e,f))\equiv PLF(f)+sizeF(f)$

$A_8: PLF(newF) \equiv 0$

$A_9: PLF(addTree(f,t,n))\equiv PLF(f)+PL(t)$

Binary Tree

Binary trees are one of the simplest forms of tree structures. They are of great importance because they offer a rather simple way to obtain a good level of performance （in average） when searching, adding or removing information.

Change tree to binary tree:

$root o root$

$1st ext{ subtree } o left child$

$right ext{ sibling } o right child$

Important properties on binary trees

  1. For a binary tree of size $n$ and height $h$, we have

$\log_2n \le h \le n-1$

  1. For a binary tree of size $n$ and path length $PL$, we have

$\sum_{i=1}^n|\log_2i|\le PL\le rac {n(n-1)} 2$

  1. Average height of a BT of size n

$h(t) = rac 1 n \sum_{e \in t}(h(e))= rac {PL(t)} {size(t)}$

ADT Binary Tree(BT)

Use boolean, element

Opreations

$new: o BT$

$element \cross BT \cross BT o BT$

$BT o element$

$left: BT o BT$

$right: BT o BT$

$contains: BT \cross element o boolean$

Preconditions

$root(t)\iff t eq new$

$left(t)\iff t eq new$

$right(t)\iff t eq new$

Axioms

$A_1: root(<r,L,R>) \equiv r$

$A_2: left(<r,L,R>) \equiv L$

$A_3: righ(<r,L,R>) \equiv R$

$A_4: contains(new,e)\equiv false$

$A_5: contains(<r,L,R>,r) \equiv true$

$A_6: contains(<r,L,R>,e)\equiv contains(L,e) \or contains(R,e)$

ADT extension BT

Use interger

Operations

$size: BT o integer$

$height: BT o integer$

$PL: BT o integer$

Axioms

$A_1:size(new) \equiv 0$

$A_2: size(<r,L,R>) \equiv 1+size(L)+size(R)$

$A_3: height(new) \equiv -1$

$A_4: height(<r,L,R>) \equiv 1+max(height(L),height(R))$

$A_5: PL(new) \equiv 0$

$A_6: PL(<r,L,R>)\equiv PL(L)+size(L)+PL(R)+size(R)$

Tree traversals

2 important approaches:

  • breadth-first

  • depth first

          A     / \    B   C   / \   \  D   E   F          / \         G   H

BF: ABCDEFGH

DF: ABDECFGH

Preorder: ABCDEFGH

Inorder: DBEAGFHC

Postorder: DEBGHFCA

BST Search Tree

In a BST, elements are stored in such a way that, for any node n,

  • all elements in the left child have a key value less than that of n,
  • all elements in the right child have a key value greater than that of n.

ADT BST extend BT

Uses Element,boolean

operations

$contains: BST imes Element o boolean$

axioms

$A_1:contains（create,e） \equiv false$

$A_2:contains（<r,L,R>,r）\equiv true$

$A_3:key（x）<key（r）\Rightarrow contains（<r,L,R>,x）= contains (L,x)$

$A_4: key（x）>key（r）\Rightarrow contains（<r,L, R>,x）= contains(R, x)$

Leaf addition in a BST

ADT extension BST

Operations

$add: BST imes e o BST$

Axioms

$A_1: add(create,e) \equiv <e,create,create>$

$A_2: add(<e,L,R>,e)\equiv <e,L,R>$

$A_3: add(t,root(t))\equiv t$

$A_4: r<e \Rightarrow add(<r,L,R>,e) \equiv (r,L,add(R,e))$

$A_5: r>e \Rightarrow add(<r,L,R>,t)\equiv (r,add(L,e),R)$

Complexity

$C(0)=0$

$C(h) = C(h-1)+3=C(h-2)+6=…=C(h-i)+3i = 3h$

Idea: use a BST to sort a set of numbers.

  1. build the BST from this set

$C(n) = \sum_{i=1}^n(add(i))=\Theta(\sum_{i=1}^n logi) = O(nlog(n))$

  1. inorder the BST to set the sorted list

$C(sort(n)) = O(nlogn)$

Extensions to ADT BST with the following operations

$lessthan(t,e) o$ a BST with all elements in $t$ that are less than $e$

$greaterthan(t,e) o$ a BST with all elements in $t$ that are greater than $e$

$rootAdd(t,e) o$ a BST with $e$ as the root and all elements of $t$

Operations:

$lessthan: BST imes Element o BST$

$greaterthan: BST imes Element o BST$

$rootAdd:BST imes Element o BST$

Axioms:

$A_1: lessthan(new,e)\equiv new$

$A_2: lessthan(<r,L,R>,r) \equiv L$

$A_3: e<r,lessthan(<r,L,R>,e) \equiv lessthan(L,e)$

$A_4: e>r, lessthan(<r,L,R>,e)\equiv <r,L,lessthan(R,e)>$

$A_5: greaterthan(new,e)\equiv new$

$A_6: greaterthan(<r,L,R>,r)\equiv R$

$A_7: r<e,greaterthan(<r,L,R>,e)\equiv greaterthan(R,e)$

$A_8:e<r,greaterthan(<r,L,R),e) \equiv <r,greaterthan(L,e),R>$

Extension to ADT BST with the 3 operations

$max(t)$: return the max element of $t$

$delmax(t)$: return $t$ minus its max element

$remove(t,e)$: return $t$ minus $e$

ADT extension BST

Operations:

$max(t): BST o element$

$delmax(t): BST o BST$

$remove(t, e): BST imes element o BST$

Preconditions

$max(t) \iff !\equiv new$

Axioms

$A_1: max (<r,L,create>)= r$

$A_2: R eq create \Rightarrow max(<r,L,R>)\equiv max(R)$

$A_3: delMax(create) \equiv create$

$A_4: delMax (<r,L,create>)\equiv L$

$A_5: L eq create \Rightarrow delMax （<r,L, R>）\equiv <r, L, delMax（R）>$

$A_6: remove（create,e）\equiv create$

$A_7: key （x）<key（r）\Rightarrow remove （<r,L, R>,x）\Rightarrow <r,remove（L, x）,R>$

$A_8: key （x）>key(r) \Rightarrow remove （<r,L, R>, x）\equiv <r,L,remove(R, x)$

$A_9: remove （<r,create,R>,r）\equiv R$

$A_{10}: L eq create \Rightarrow remove （<r, L, R>,r）\equiv <max （L）,delMax （L）,R>$

entry.title: French Week2&3
entry.content:
Building Basic Sentences

Statement

Noun [N] or Pronoun [P]+Verb [V] + Adjective [Adj] or Adverb [Adv] or Noun [N]

Ma chambre [N] est [V] grande [Adj] = My bedroom is big.

Mon frère [N] aime [V] le fromage [N] = My brother likes cheese.

Elle [P] travaille [V] trop [Adv] = She works too much.

Negative Statement

ne (+verbe) pas” is the typical form to make a negative sentence. The verb located in between “ne” and “pas” is conjugated. When the verb starts with a vowel, “ne” becomes “**n’**”

Question

  • “quand” [ken]= when
  • “qui” [kee] = who
  • “quoi” [kwa] = what
  • “où” [oo] = where
  • “pourquoi” [poorkwa] = why
  • “combien” [konbee-un] = how many/much

Formal: Où habites-tu? [MI+verb+subject]

Informal: Où est-ce que tu habites? [MI+est-ce que+subject+verb]

Familiar: Tu habits Où? [subject+verb+MI]

Articles

ArticleMaleFemalePlural
DefineLeLaLes
IndefiniteUnUneDes
PartitifsDuDe La—

À+le = Au, À + les = Aux

De+le = Du, De+les = Des

une, une, des changes to de when used in negative sentences

entry.title: Linear Algebra Week1&2
entry.content:
Data Science: Math + CS + Business

AI: Let the computer make an intellectual task as well as a human being (e.g.: Chess game [Decison Tree])

Machine Learning: Teaching skills via data. Data learn how to make the task itself via a specific algorithm (e.g.: Netflix recommendation engine)

Neural Network: Each point link each other and each of them is a small computer

Deep Learning: Neural Network + several layers in the middle

To have a product between a matrix and a vector, make sure the number of columns of the matrix is equal to the number of components of the vector

A diagonal matrix is a special type of square matrix in which all the entries outside the main diagonal are zero. The main diagonal runs from the top-left corner to the bottom-right corner of the matrix, and it contains all the non-zero elements of the matrix. In mathematical notation, if you have a square matrix A with elements $a_{ij}$, where i and j are row and column indices, a diagonal matrix can be represented as:
$$
A = \begin{bmatrix}
a_{11} & 0 & 0 & \ldots & 0 \\
0 & a_{22} & 0 & \ldots & 0 \\
0 & 0 & a_{33} & \ldots & 0 \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
0 & 0 & 0 & \ldots & a_{nn} \\
\end{bmatrix}
$$
Normalized vector: Normalizing a vector means scaling it to have a length (magnitude) of 1 while preserving its direction.

For example:
$$
\text{for}\quad u = (1,3) \
|u| = (\frac 1 {\sqrt {10}},\frac 3 {\sqrt {10}})
$$

Colinear vectors

2 vectors ($\vec u,\vec v$) are collinear if and only if $\exists k \in \mathbb{R} | \vec u = k\vec v \quad \text{or}\quad \vec v =k\vec u$. In the graph, $\vec u, \vec v$ are colinear, they are parallel.

$\vec 0$ is colinear to each vector.

Orthogonal vectors

$\vec u,\vec v $ are orthogonal vectors if $\vec u \cdot \vec v =0$

Product of Matrix

for $m \in M_{\displaystyle{m,n}^{(\mathbb{R})}}$ and $n \in M_{\displaystyle{n,p}^{(\mathbb{R})}}$, $m \cdot n = C_{\displaystyle{m,p}^{(\mathbb{R})}}$

$\det(A) \neq 0 \Leftrightarrow A\cdot A^{-1}=A^{-1} \cdot A = I(\text{Indentity matrix})$ and $I\cdot A =A\cdot I = A$

For the common Identity matrix:

$$I_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1&0\\0&1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$I_3 = \begin{bmatrix}1&0&0\\0&1&0\\0&0&1\end{bmatrix}$$

In general, $A \cdot B \neq B \cdot A$

Determinant of Matrix

Only for square matrix, In the $R^2$, the determinant is the area, in the $R^3 \quad\text{or}\quad R^n$, it is the volume.

for $$A = \begin{bmatrix} a b \\ c d\end{bmatrix}$$, $\det(A) = ad-bc$

for the higher dimension matrix, such as

$$\begin{align*}
\text{det}(A) &= \begin{vmatrix}
a & b & c & d \\
e & f & g & h \\
i & j & k & l \\
m & n & o & p
\end{vmatrix} \\
&= a \begin{vmatrix}
f & g & h \\
j & k & l \\
n & o & p
\end{vmatrix} - b \begin{vmatrix}
e & g & h \\
i & k & l \\
m & o & p
\end{vmatrix} + c \begin{vmatrix}
e & f & h \\
i & j & l \\
m & n & p
\end{vmatrix} - d \begin{vmatrix}
e & f & g \\
i & j & k \\
m & n & o
\end{vmatrix}
\end{align*}$$

If A has a row that is all zeros, then $\det A = 0$.

$\det AB = (\det A)(\det B)$

If there are 2 same rows in the matrix A, the $\det A = 0$

Inverse of Matrix

$ A^{-1} = (adj A)/(\det A)$

For a $3*3$ Matrix

  1. Check if the Matrix is Invertible (Non-Singular)
  2. Calculate the minor of the origin
  3. Find the cofactor
  4. Find the adjoint
  5. Calute the inverse

Eg:
$$
\begin{align*}
&\text{find the inverse matrix of A}\\
&A =
\begin{vmatrix}
2&-1&3\\
0&5&2\\
1&-1&-2\\
\end{vmatrix}\\
&\text{1. Check the determinate:}\\
&\det(A) = -33\\
&\text{2. Find the minor:}\\
&M_{11} =
\begin{vmatrix}
5&2\\
-1&-2
\end{vmatrix} = 5*(-2)-2*(-1) = -8\\
&M_{12} = -2\\
&M_{13} = -5\\
&M_{21} = 5\\
&M_{22} = -7\\
&M_{23} = -1\\
&M_{31} = -17\\
&M_{32} = 4\\
&M_{33} = 10\\
&\text{So, the minor of A is:}\\
&B =
\begin{vmatrix}
-8&-2&-5\\
5&-7&-1\\
-17&4&10\\
\end{vmatrix}\\
&\text{3.the cofactor:}\\
&C=
\begin{vmatrix}
-8&2&-5\\
-5&-7&1\\
-17&-4&10\\
\end{vmatrix}\\
&\text{4. find the adjoint:}\\
&adjB = C^T =
\begin{vmatrix}
-8&-5&-17\\
2&-7&-4\\
-5&1&10\\
\end{vmatrix}\\
&\text{5. Calculate the inverse:}\\
&A^{-1} = -1/33 *
\begin{vmatrix}
-8&2&-5\\
-5&-7&1\\
-17&-4&10\\
\end{vmatrix}
\end{align*}
$$

Eigenvalues & Eigenvectors of a square matrix

Only for square matrix, $Au = \lambda u$, $\lambda$ is the eigenvalue of $A$ and u is the eigenvector of $A$

How to calculate?

  • Solve the characteristic polynomial to find the eigenvalues: $\det(A-\lambda I) =0$

  • For every $\lambda_i$ (Eigenvalues), solve $Au=\lambda_iu$ to find the eigenvecdors

$$
\begin{aligned}
Au &= \lambda u \\
Au-\lambda Iu &= 0\\
(A-\lambda I)u &= 0\\
\det (A-\lambda I)&=0
\end{aligned}
$$

**N.B.: **

  1. For the matrix $A$, it may have more than one eigenvalues and for each eigenvalue, it may have many eigenvectors.

  2. Eigenvectors corresponding to different eigenvalues are linearly independent

entry.title: Data Privacy
entry.content:
What is data?

Data are the facts and statistics collected together for reference or analysis according to Oxford Dict.

There is a DIWD pyramid to help us understand and use data, as following:

Data (Base, 1st): represents raw, unprocessed facts and figures.

Information (2nd): It results from organizing and processing data to provide context and relevance. Information answers the questions of who, what, where, and when

Knowledge (3rd): Knowledge is a step higher than information. It goes beyond “what” and “where” to address “how” and “why.” Knowledge is derived from analyzing and understanding information in a way that allows you to make decisions, solve problems, or create new insights.

Wisdom (Top, 4th): It involves the ability to make sound judgments and decisions based on knowledge and experience. Wisdom goes beyond just understanding “how” and “why” to apply that understanding in a way that is ethical and beneficial.

There are two main categories of data: quantitive data and qualitative data. The data that can be understood or read by humans but may not be accepted by machines, we say the former is unstructured data and say the machine-friendly data is structured data.

Data Privacy by Design

Users are concerned that their collected data be safe and well-storage, they expect to stay in control of their personal data.

The legal laws and obligations are set up based on this point like GDPR, HIGBA, GLBA, etc.

The fundamental principles for data privacy are:

  • Proactive not Reactive; Preventive not remedial

  • Privacy as the default setting

  • Privacy Embedded into Design

  • Full functionality - Positive-Sum, not Zero-sum

  • End-to-end security – Full lifecycle protection

  • Visibility and transparency – keep it open

  • Respect for user privacy – keep it user-centric

Overall goal: Minimizing privacy risks and trust other consumptions placed on other entities.

Main Strategies:

  • Minimize Collection
  • Minimize Disclosure
  • Minimize Linkability
  • Minimize Centralization
  • Minimize Replication
  • Minimize Retention

LINDDUN Privacy engineering framework:

  • Linkability: Linkability refers to the ability to link various pieces of information or actions to a single entity or individual. This can be a concern for privacy. For example, in the context of online advertising, if a user’s online activity across multiple websites is linkable to their real-world identity, it can lead to privacy issues.
  • Identifiability: Identifiability is closely related to linkability. It involves the extent to which an entity or individual can be identified based on the data or information available. An example of this is the use of anonymized medical records. While the records are anonymized, there is a risk of re-identifying individuals based on specific details.
  • Non-repudiation: Non-repudiation ensures that a party cannot deny the authenticity of a transaction or action they have taken. In the context of digital signatures, non-repudiation ensures that a person who has digitally signed a document cannot later claim they did not do it.
  • Detectability: Detectability refers to the ability to detect certain actions or events. For example, in cybersecurity, the ability to detect unauthorized access or suspicious activity on a network is crucial for identifying and responding to potential threats.
  • Disclosure of Information: This principle concerns the intentional or unintentional disclosure of sensitive information. For instance, an accidental data breach by a company leading to the exposure of customer data is a disclosure of information that can have legal and ethical implications.
  • Unawareness: Unawareness refers to the state of not being aware of certain actions or events. For example, a user might be unaware that their online behavior is being tracked for advertising purposes, which can raise privacy concerns.
  • Non-compliance: Non-compliance refers to the failure to adhere to established rules, regulations, or security policies. In the context of data protection regulations like GDPR, non-compliance can result in legal consequences for organizations that do not follow the required data protection practices.

entry.title: Complexity
entry.content:
Intro to Complexity

In CS, we have different ways to solve the same question. Depending on the chosen criteria, one of them is the “best” solution. An algorithm is defined as complex based on the amount of Space and Time it consumes. Hence the Complexity of an algorithm refers to the measure of the time it will need to execute and get the expected output and the Space it will need to store all the data (input, temporary data, and output). Hence these two factors define the efficiency of an algorithm.

  • Time Factor: Time is measured by counting the number of key operations such as comparisons in the sorting algorithm.
  • Space Factor: Space is measured by counting the maximum memory space the algorithm requires to run/execute.

Analysis of Complexity

Asymptotic notation is a way to describe the running time or space complexity of an algorithm. The three most commonly used notations are Big O, Big Omega, and Big Theta.

Big O Notation: Big $O$ notation represents the upper bound of the running time of an algorithm. Therefore, it gives the worst-case complexity of an algorithm.

Big $\Omega$ Notation: Big $\Omega$ notation represents the lower bound of the running time of an algorithm. Thus, it provides the best-case complexity of an algorithm.

Big $\Theta$ Notation: Big $\Theta$ represents the upper and the lower bound of the running time of an algorithm, it is used for analyzing the average-case complexity of an algorithm.

In real life, we will consider the worst situation when analyzing an algorithm because it is harder and impossible (sometimes) to consider the other two.

Mathematic Algorithm

We can represent the asymptotic notation in a mathematical way with the help of the Limit.

$f(n) \in O (g(n)) \Rightarrow \displaystyle \lim_{n \to \infty} f(n)/g(n) \in [0,\infty) $, in other words, f(n) is the worst case of g(n)

$f(n) \in \Omega(g(n)) \Rightarrow \displaystyle \lim_{n \to \infty} f(n)/g(n) \in (0,\infty] $

$f(n) \in \Theta(g(n)) \Rightarrow \displaystyle \lim_{n \to \infty} f(n)/g(n) \in (0,\infty) $

Properties

General Properties: If $f(n)$ is $O(g(n))$ then $a \cdot f(n)$ is also $O(g(n))$ where $a$ is a constant.

Transitive Properties: if $f(n) \in O(g(n)), g(n) \in O(h(n))$, then $f(n) \in O(h(n))$.

Reflexive Properties: $f(n) \in O(f(n))$, also $f(n) \in \Theta(f(n))$

Symmetric Properties: if $f(n) \in \Theta(g(n))$, then $g(n) \in \Theta(f(n)$

Transpose Symmetric Properties: if $f(n) \in O(g(n))$, then $g(n) \in \Theta(f(n))$

Calculation of Time Complexity

We usually focus on time complexity.

$O(1) < O(\log n) < O(n) < O(n \log n) < O(n^2) < O(n^3) < O(2^n) < O(n!) < O(n^n)$

Constant Time Complexity $O(1)$:

The time complexity of a function (or set of statements) is considered as $O(1)$ if it doesn’t contain a loop, recursion, and call to any other non-constant time function.

Linear Time Complexity $O(n)$:

The Time Complexity of a loop is considered as $O(n)$ if the loop variables are incremented/decremented by a constant amount.

Quadratic Time Complexity $O(n^n)$:

The time complexity is defined as an algorithm whose performance is directly proportional to the squared size of the input data, as in nested loops it is equal to the number of times the innermost statement is executed.

Logarithmic Time Complexity $O(\log n)$:

The time Complexity of a loop is considered as $O(\log n)$ if the loop variables are divided/multiplied by a constant amount. Also for recursive calls in the recursive function, the Time Complexity is considered as $O(\log n)$.

Algorithms Cheat Sheet:

AlgorithmBest CaseAverage CaseWorst Case
Selection SortO(n^2)O(n^2)O(n^2)
Bubble SortO(n)O(n^2)O(n^2)
Insertion SortO(n)O(n^2)O(n^2)
Tree SortO(nlogn)O(nlogn)O(n^2)
Radix SortO(dn)O(dn)O(dn)
Merge SortO(nlogn)O(nlogn)O(nlogn)
Heap SortO(nlogn)O(nlogn)O(nlogn)
Quick SortO(nlogn)O(nlogn)O(n^2)
Bucket SortO(n+k)O(n+k)O(n^2)
Counting SortO(n+k)O(n+k)O(n+k)

Calculation of complexity

The complexity of a sequence is the sum of each statement included in the sequence

For the loop, the complexity $C = \displaystyle\sum_i C_i$, $i$ is the index of the statement inside of the loop.

For the condition, the complexity:

  • worst case: $C_i=C_{cond} + max(C_{if}+C_{else})$

  • best case: $C_i=C_{cond} + min(C_{if}+C_{else})$

  • average case: $C_i=C_{cond} + P*C_{if}+(1-P)*C_{else}$

Complexity of recursion

To determine a recurrence equation, we need to find a relationship between the initial call and the closer call to the stop.

Example:

Consider this recursion code:

$$
\begin{cases}
C_{0} = C_{1} = 0\\
C_{n} = C_{n-1} + 1 = C_{n-2}+1+1 = … =n-1
\end{cases}
$$

Divide and conquer

$ C_n = a C_{\frac n b}+\theta (n^c)$

Master theorem:

  • $a<b^c: C_n = \theta (n^c)$
  • $a=b^c: C_n = \theta (n^c\log n)$
  • $a>b^c: C_n = \theta (n^{\log_b^a})$

For ipow: $C_n = C_{\frac n 2}+1 = \theta (\log n)$

For merge sort: $C_n = 2(\frac n 2)+n = \theta(n\log n)$

For Fibonacci sequence:

A Fibonacci sequence has the formation as follows:
$$
\begin{cases}
f_0 = f_1 = 1\\
f_n = f_{n-1} +f_{n-2}
\end{cases}
$$

$$
\begin{cases}
C_0 = 0\\
C_1 = 0\\
C_n =1+ C_{n-1} +C_{n-2}
\end{cases}
\Rightarrow\\
let\quad D_n = C_n+1 \quad(\text{substitute })\Rightarrow\\D_n = D_{n-1}+D_{n-2}\\
$$

Amazing, it is still a Fibonacci sequence! Suppose $D_n = \alpha^n$ , then we can get $\alpha^n = \alpha^{n-1}+\alpha^{n-2}$, suppose $n=2$, then $\alpha = \frac {1\pm \sqrt{5}} 2$, that means $D_n = ({\frac {1\pm \sqrt{5}} 2})^n$.

To avoid recomputation, let’s reform the code like this:

$$
\begin{cases}
C_0 &= C_1 =0\\
C_n &= C_{n-1}+1\\
&=C_{n-2}+2\\
&=C_{n-3}+3\\
&=\cdot\cdot\cdot \\
&=C_{n-k} + k\\
&=\theta(C_n)
\end{cases}
$$

entry.title: French Week1
entry.content:
Numbers

Num(1-10)FrenchNum(11-20)French
0Zéro11Onze
1Un12Douze
2Duex13Treize
3Trois14Quatorze
4Quatre15Quinze
5Cinq16Seize
6Six17Dix-sept
7Sept18Dix-huit
8Huit19Dix-neuf
9Neuf20Vingt
10Dix
Num(<100)FrenchNum(>=100)French
21Vingt et tun100Cent
22-29Vingt-deux … Vingt-neuf101Cent et un
30Trente102Cent-deux
31Trente et un200Duex Cents
32-39Trente-deux … Trent-neuf1,000Mille
40Quarante1,200Mille deux-cents
41Quarante et un1,900Mille neuf-cents
42-49Quarante-deux … Quarante-neuf1,910Mille neuf-cent-dix
50Cinquante1,000,000Un million
51Cinquate et un5,000,000Cinq millions
52-59Cinquate-deux … Cinquate-neuf
60Soixante
